style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">CanBusPorts</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">}</span></span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><h4>Auto-creating <span>n-ary interface </span>definition elements via the Extract Definition command</h4><p><span>To auto-create n-ary interface definition elements via the Extract Definition command</span></p><hr /><ul><li><span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Extract Definition and Usage" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Extract Definition and Usage]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">page.</span></span></li></ul><h4>Creating n-ary interface definitions via the view palette</h4><p>To create n-ary interface definitions via the view palette</p><hr /><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the view palette, under the Connectors group, click the<strong> interface def</strong> button. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="769c3a3a-7b27-471d-b151-165748c95ab6"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If the needed button is not displayed, click the down-facing arrow next to either the <span><strong>interface </strong>or the <strong>n-ary interface </strong></span>button and select the needed button in the dropdown menu.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li>Click in the view pane where you want to create the interface def symbol. The interface def symbol is created containing two <strong>end </strong>element symbols.<ol><li data-uuid="c08d6faa-201e-42d6-af51-9f20bd1e4288">Create additional ends:<ol><li data-uuid="7a64217e-9f55-4afd-8470-2e739d6f2e35">Via the <strong>Create Element </strong>button's <strong>end </strong>command. See the procedure<span> </span><ac:link ac:anchor="Creating elements via compartments"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Compartments" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Creating elements via compartments]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><span> </span>on the<span> </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Compartments" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Compartments]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><span> </span>page. </li><li data-uuid="39d6b358-66ce-4d79-a18c-5d49d4e43a4f">In the view palette, under the Items/Ports/Attributes group, click the<strong> port</strong> button. Click on the interface def symbol to create a port for it.</li></ol></li></ol></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=298093490 space=UAF2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00114: (2026x) Occurrences

- page_id: `298093490`
- space_key: `UAF2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF2P/pages/298093490/2026x+Occurrences
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-10-14T15:14:24.569+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Common elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An occurrence is used to designate something that has an extent in time, which is the occurrence's lifetime. Occurrences can be structural (parts, items) and behavioral (actions). There are two types of suboccurrences:

- Timeslice - represents a portion of the occurrence's lifetime.
- Snapshot - is a time slice with zero duration, representing an occurrence at a specific point in time.

Any occurrence can be specified as an individual, representing an entity with a distinct identity within a modeled system, allowing for its full or partial characterization.

##### Creating occurrence usage and definition elements

###### Creating occurrence usage and definition elements via the Textual Editor

To create occurrence usage and definition elements via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare one of the following keywords:
  1. *occurrence def*for an occurrence definition.
  2. occurrence for an occurrence usage.
2. Name the element.
3. (Optional) Specify the occurrence features in the element body.
4. Click the Synchronize button. occurrencedefCruiseControlInteraction;// occurrence definition 
occurrencecruiseController;// occurrence usage

###### Creating occurrence usage and definition elements via the Create Element command

To create occurrence usage and definition elements via the Create Element command

1. In the Containment tree, right-click an element, and in the shortcut menu, click **Create Element**.
2. In the Create Element dialog, select one of the following:
  1. occurrence def to create an occurrence definition element.
  2. occurrence to create an occurrence usage element.
  3. event occurrence to create an event occurrence usage element.
3. Name the element.
4. (Optional) Specify the occurrence features via the Textual Editor.

##### Specifying or creating elements as timeslices

###### Specifying or creating elements as timeslices via the Textual Editor

To specify or create elements as timeslices via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, declare the *timeslice* keyword prior to the declaration of the element that you want to specify as a timeslice.
2. Click the **Synchronize**button. timeslicepartcarInMotion;

###### Specifying elements as timeslices via the shortcut menu

To specify elements as timeslices via the shortcut menu

- Right-click a usage element in the Containment tree or its symbol in a view and in its shortcut menu, in the Properties group, click **Portion Kind**, and in its submenu, click **timeslice** .

###### Creating elements as timeslices via the view palette

To create elements as timeslices via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Items/Ports/Attributes group, do one of the following:
  1. To create an occurrence usage timeslice or one that will correspond to the element kind for which the timeslice will be created:
    1. Click the**timeslice** button.
    2. Click in the view pane or the element symbol for which you want the timeslice to be created:
      1. Creating an element on the empty space of the view pane creates an occurrence usage element as a timeslice by default.
      2. Creating an element on another element symbol creates a timeslice for it:
        1. If the element symbol is a definition, the created timeslice is also a usage (e.g. part) that corresponds to the definition element for which the timeslice is created (e.g. part def).
        2. If the element is a usage, the created timeslice is also a usage of the same kind as the element for which the timeslice is created.
  2. To specify the timeslice's element kind:
    1. Click the down-facing arrow next to the timeslice button, and in its dropdown menu, click any timeslice... and select the specific element kind you need.
    2. Click in the view pane or the element symbol for which you want to create the timeslice.

If a timeslice element is created for an element specified as an individual, the timeslice is also created as an individual by default.

##### Specifying or creating elements as snapshots

###### Specifying or creating elements as snapshotsvia the Textual Editor

To specify or create elements as snapshots via Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, declare the *snapshot* keyword prior to the declaration of the element that you want to specify as a snapshot.
2. Click the **Synchronize**button. snapshotpartcarInMotion;

###### Specifying elements as snapshots via the shortcut menu

To specify elements as snapshots via the shortcut menu

- Right-click a usage element in the Containment tree or its symbol in a view and in its shortcut menu, in the Properties group, click **Portion Kind**, and in its submenu, click **snapshot** .

###### Creating elements assnapshots via the view palette

To create elements as snapshots via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Items/Ports/Attributes group, do one of the following:
  1. To create an occurrence usage snapshot or one that will correspond to the element kind for which the snapshot will be created:
    1. Click the**snapshot** button. If it is not displayed, click the down-facing arrow next to the **timeslice** button and click the **snapshot** button in the dropdown menu.
    2. Click in the view pane or the element symbol for which you want the snapshot to be created:
      1. Creating an element on the empty space of the view pane creates an occurrence usage element as a snapshot by default.
      2. Creating an element on another element symbol creates a snapshot for it:
        1. If the element symbol is a definition, the created snapshot is also a usage (e.g. part) that corresponds to the definition element for which the snapshot is created (e.g. part def).
        2. If the element is a usage, the created snapshot is also a usage of the same kind as the element for which the snapshot is created.
  2. To specify the snapshot's element kind:
    1. Click the down-facing arrow next to the **timeslice** button, and in its dropdown menu, click **any snapshot...** and select the specific element kind you need.
    2. Click in the view pane or the element symbol for which you want to create the snapshot.

If a snapshot element is created for an element specified as an individual, the snapshot is also created as an individual by default.

##### Specifying elements as individuals

###### Specifying elements as individuals via the Textual Editor

To specify elements as individuals via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, declare the *individual* keyword prior to the declaration of the element that you want to designate as an individual.
2. Click the **Synchronize**button. individualpartdefMyCar:>Car;

###### Specifying elements as individuals via the element shortcut menu

To specify elements as individuals via the element shortcut menu

- Right-click an element in the Containment tree or its symbol in a view and in its shortcut menu, in the Properties group, select the Is Individual property.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=298094064 space=UAF2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00115: (2026x) Opening views

- page_id: `298094064`
- space_key: `UAF2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF2P/pages/298094064/2026x+Opening+views
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-10T18:04:10.896+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) View
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

See the procedures on this page for opening views.

##### Opening views

###### Opening views via the Containment tree

Opening vews via the Containment tree

1. Select the view element in the Containment tree and do one of the following:
  1. Double-click the view element.
  2. Right-click the view element and in the shortcut menu, click Open or Open in New Tab .
  3. On the keyboard, press Ctrl+Enter or Enter . **Open**is equivalent to **Ctrl+Enter**. The commands open the selected view in place of the currently active view.**Open in New Tab**is equivalent to **Enter**. The commands open the selected view in a new view tab.

###### Opening views via the Textual Editor

Opening views via the Textual Editor

- In the Textual Editor, right-click the view you want to open and in the shortcut menu, click Open View .

###### Opening views via the view element symbol in a view

Opening views via the view element symbol in a view

1. Select the view element symbol in a view and do one of the following:
  1. Double-click the view element symbol.
  2. Right-click the view element symbol and in the shortcut menu, click Open or Open in New Tab .

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>See the procedures on this page for opening views.</p><h3>Opening views</h3><h4>Opening views via the Containment tree</h4><p>Opening vews via the Containment tree</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the view element in the Containment tree and do one of the following:<ol><li>Double-click the view element.</li><li>Right-click the view element and in the shortcut menu, click <strong>Open</strong> or <strong>Open in New Tab</strong>.</li><li>On the keyboard, press <strong>Ctrl+Enter </strong>or <strong>Enter</strong>. <br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6392ba39-8ce6-4a63-97fc-6dc431efd7dd"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><strong>Open </strong>is equivalent to <strong>Ctrl+Enter</strong>. The commands open the selected view in place of the currently active view. </li><li><strong>Open in New Tab </strong>is equivalent to <strong>Enter</strong>. The commands open the selected view in a new view tab.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol></li></ol><h4>Opening views via the Textual Editor</h4><p>Opening views via the Textual Editor</p><hr /><ul><li data-uuid="ededb690-d68f-49f7-af40-8046bad289af">In the Textual Editor, right-click the view you want to open and in the shortcut menu, click <strong>Open View</strong>.</li></ul><h4>Opening views via the view element symbol in a view</h4><p>Opening views via the view element symbol in a view</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the view element symbol in a view and do one of the following:<ol><li>Double-click the view element symbol.</li><li>Right-click the view element symbol and in the shortcut menu, click <strong>Open</strong> or <strong>Open in New Tab</strong>.</li></ol></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=298093477 space=UAF2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00116: (2026x) Package

- page_id: `298093477`
- space_key: `UAF2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF2P/pages/298093477/2026x+Package
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-12-03T08:48:55.959+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Common elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Packages are namespaces that contain member elements that may be owned or unowned. It is used as a container for organizing other elements within the model. For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Key concepts' space='UAF2P'] page.Additionally, a package can import members of another package as unowned members. You can also filter imported elements based on certain conditions.

##### Creating package elements

###### Creating a package element via the Textual Editor

To create a package element via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the keyword:
  1. *package*.
  2. *library package* .
2. Specify the element name.
3. (Optional) [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Package' space='UAF2P'] .
4. Click the Synchronize button. library package Policies; // library package elementpackage'Vehicle Features'{ // package element 
privateimportPolicies::**;filter@SysML::Systems::PartUsage;}

###### Creating a package element via the Create Element command

To create a package element via the Create Element command

1. In the Containment tree, right-click a root namespace, and in the shortcut menu, click Create Element .
2. In the Create Element dialog, select one of the following:
  1. package to create a package element.
  2. library package to create a library package element.
3. Name the element.
4. (Optional) [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Package' space='UAF2P'] .

##### Specifying packages

###### Importing elements to packages

An import relationship allows one package to import memberships from another package. The member elements from imported memberships become (unowned) members of the importing package in addition to being members of the imported package. In particular, this allows members of the imported package to be referenced in textual notations within the scope of the importing package without having to qualify the member names with the name of the imported package.

- For more information and procedures, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Importing and filtering elements' space='UAF2P'] procedure on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Importing and filtering elements' space='UAF2P'] page.

###### Specifying element import filters

Packages have the capability to filter elements upon import based on certain conditions, usually defined in terms of the metadata provided by annotations of those elements. Only elements that meet all filter conditions become imported members of the packages.

- For more information and procedures, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Importing and filtering elements' space='UAF2P'] section on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Importing and filtering elements' space='UAF2P'] page.

###### Package members display in symbolic views

- To display package members in the Members compartment of the package symbol, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Compartments' space='UAF2P'].
- To control whether the Members compartment displays only owned members or imported ones as well, use the **Show Only Owned** [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Modifying styles of individual symbols' space='UAF2P'].

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Packages are namespaces that contain member elements that may be owned or unowned. It is used as a container for organizing other elements within the model. <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">For more information, see the <ac:link ac:anchor="Package"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Key concepts" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Key concepts]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page. </span>Additionally, a package can import members of another package as unowned members. You can also filter imported elements based on certain conditions.<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span></span></p><h3 class="auto-cursor-target">Creating package elements</h3><h4>Creating a package element via the Textual Editor</h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To create a package element via the Textual Editor</span></p><hr /><ol><li>In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the keyword: <ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span> </span><em>package</em>.</p></li><li><span> <em>library package</em></span>.</li></ol></li><li>Specify the element name.</li><li>(Optional) <ac:link ac:anchor="Specifying packages"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Specify the package]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Package" /></ac:link>.</li><li>Click the <strong>Synchronize </strong>button.<ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="eb178161-53d8-4310-9951-d1d380111bdd"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">library package</span> <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">Policies</span>;  <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// library package element</span>  </span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">package<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">'Vehicle Features'</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span> </span>{  <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// package element</span></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">   <span> </span></span>private<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span> </span></span>import<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Policies</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">::**; <br /></span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">   <span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">filter</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span> </span>@</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">SysML::Systems::PartUsage</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;<span> <br /></span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">} </span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><h4>Creating a package element via the Create Element command</h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To create a package element via the Create Element command</span></p><hr /><ol><li>In the Containment tree, right-click a root namespace, and in the shortcut menu, click <strong>Create Element</strong>.</li><li>In the <strong>Create Element</strong> dialog, select one of the following:<ol><li><strong>package </strong>to create a package element.</li><li><strong>library package </strong>to create a library package element.</li></ol></li><li>Name the element.</li><li>(Optional) <ac:link ac:anchor="Specifying packages"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Specify the package]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Package" /></ac:link>.</li></ol><h3 class="auto-cursor-target">Specifying packages</h3><h4>Importing elements to packages</h4><p><span>An import relationship allows one package to import memberships from another package. Only elements that meet all filter conditions become imported members of the packages. </p><ul><li><span>For more information and procedures, see the <ac:link ac:anchor="Element import filtering"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Importing and filtering elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Element import filtering]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> section on the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Importing and filtering elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Importing and filtering elements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</span></li></ul><h4><span>Package members display in symbolic views</span></h4><ul><li data-uuid="7d57743d-bdc0-4eed-8626-602323185313"><span>To display package members in the Members compartment of the package symbol, <ac:link ac:anchor="Displaying and suppressing compartments"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Compartments" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[display the compartment]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</span></li><li data-uuid="0f7b1a13-9ae9-458c-9368-ceec5c91e2c7"><span>To control whether the Members compartment displays only owned members or imported ones as well, use the <strong>Show Only Owned</strong> <ac:link ac:anchor="The Symbol Style dialog"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Modifying styles of individual symbols" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[symbol style]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</span></li></ul><p><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=298093918 space=UAF2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00117: (2026x) Parallel states

- page_id: `298093918`
- space_key: `UAF2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF2P/pages/298093918/2026x+Parallel+states
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-10-14T15:23:07.138+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Behavior > (2026x) States
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A state definition or usage may hierarchically contain state usages in its body. The performance of these substates depends on whether the containing state is parallel or non-parallel:

- In non-parallel states (the default), substate performances occur sequentially, meaning only one substate is active at any given time. Transitions control the activation of these substates.
- In parallel states (triggered by adding the keyword parallel before the body), substates run concurrently, with their performances overlapping in time. No transitions are allowed between concurrent states.

Contained substates may also decompose further, employing transitions in sequential states.

##### Specifying parallel states

###### Specifying parallel states via the Textual Editor

To specify parallel states via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, declare the keyword parallel before the state's body.
2. Click the Synchronize button.

stateonparallel{ // The following substates are performed concurrentlystatespeed{ ...} // Any contained substates are performed sequentially 
stateheat{ ...} // Any contained substates are performed sequentially 
}

###### Specifying parallel states via the element shortcut menu

To specify parallel states via the element shortcut menu

- Right-click a state element in the Containment tree or its symbol in a view and in its shortcut menu, in the Properties group, select the Is Parallel property.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

Transitions control the activation of these substates.</li><li>In <strong>parallel states</strong> (triggered by adding the keyword <em>parallel</em> before the body), substates run concurrently, with their performances overlapping in time. No transitions are allowed between concurrent states.</li></ul><p>Contained substates may also decompose further, employing transitions in sequential states.</p><h3>Specifying parallel <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=298093674 space=UAF2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00118: (2026x) Part decomposition

- page_id: `298093674`
- space_key: `UAF2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF2P/pages/298093674/2026x+Part+decomposition
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-10-14T15:18:09.996+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Structure > (2026x) Parts
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Part definitions and part usages can be decomposed allowing you to build elaborate model structures composed of related parts. You can create a parts tree representing a composition of a part from other parts.

##### Decomposing part definitions

You can represent the next level of decomposition of a part definition using the feature typing (*defined by*) relationship.

###### Decomposing part definitions via the Textual Editor

To decompose part definitions via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, identify the part usage that you want to define, nested within a part definition.
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Feature typing' space='UAF2P'] .

partdefVehicle{ 
partengine:Engine; 
parttransmission:Transmission; 
partdriveshaft:Driveshaft; 
partrearAxleAssembly:RearAxleAssembly;// part usage, owned by a part definition, defined by another part definitionpartfuelTank:FuelTank; 
} 
partdefRearAxleAssembly{ 
partdifferential:Differential; 
partrearAxle:Axle; 
partrearWheel1:Wheel; 
partrearWheel2:Wheel; 
}

###### Displaying decompositions of part definitions in a view

To display decompositions of part definitions in a view

- In a view, select the part definition symbol and the part usage symbol it defines, then use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='UAF2P'] command under the In-Between Relationships category . The feature typing relationship is displayed.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Decomposing part usages

You can display a parts tree representing a composition of a part from other parts.

###### Decomposing part usages via the Textual Editor

To decompose part usages via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, within the body of the part you want to decompose, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating parts' space='UAF2P'] .
2. Click the Synchronize button.

partvehicle_b : Vehicle{ // the owning part usage with nested part usages 
partengine:Engine{ 
partcyl:Cylinder; 
} 
parttransmission:Transmission; 
partfuelTank:FuelTank; 
partrearAxleAssembly:AxleAssembly; 
}

###### Decomposing part usages via a view by creating a composite part

To decompose part usages via a view by creating a composite part

1. In a view, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating parts' space='UAF2P'] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='UAF2P'] the part for which you want to create a composite part.
2. Create a composite part in one of the following ways:
  1. Via the view palette (part symbol option):
    1. In the view palette, under the parts group, click the part button to create a part usage element.
    2. Click on the part symbol displayed in the view pane to create a part owned by another.
  2. Via the view palette (feature membership option):
    1. In the view palette, under the Other Relationships group, click the feature membership button.
    2. First, click on the part symbol displayed in the view pane and then click elsewhere in the view pane to create a part owned by another.
  3. Via the smart manipulator (feature membership option):
    1. Click the part symbol for which you want to create a composite part.
    2. In its smart manipulator, click the feature membership icon , then click elsewhere in the view pane to create a part owned by another.

###### Decomposing part usages via a view by designating an existing part as a composite one

To decompose part usages via a view by designating an existing part as a composite one

1. In a view, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating parts' space='UAF2P'] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='UAF2P'] the parts you want to designate as the owner and the owned.
2. Designate an existing part as owned by another in one of the following ways:
  1. Via the view palette (feature membership option):
    1. In the view palette, under the Other Relationships group, click the feature membership button.
    2. First, click on the part symbol you want to designate as the owner, then the one you want to designate as the owned one.
  2. Via the smart manipulator (feature membership option):
    1. Click the part symbol you want to designate as the owner.
    2. In its smart manipulator, click the feature membership icon, then click on the part you want to designate as the owned one.

###### Displaying decompositions of part usages in a view

To display decompositions of part usages in a view

- In a view, select the owning part usage symbol and use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='UAF2P'] command under the Tree-View Style category. The feature membership and the owned features (part usages) are displayed.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Reusing and modifying the parts tree

A part can subset another part, facilitating parts tree reuse and modification. The subsetting part inherits the part decomposition and other features. You can then modify the inherited parts and features through redefinition and subsetting in addition to adding new parts.

###### Reusing and modifying the parts tree via the Textual Editor

To reuse and modify the parts tree via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating parts' space='UAF2P'] you want to reuse, then [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Subsetting' space='UAF2P'] it.
2. (Optional) [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Redefinition' space='UAF2P'] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Subsetting' space='UAF2P'] the part features in the element body.
3. Click the Synchronize button.

partvehicle_b: Vehicle{// subsetted part 
partengine:Engine{ 
partcyl[4..8] :Cylinder; 
} 
parttransmission:Transmission; 
partfuelTank:FuelTank; 
partrearAxleAssembly:AxleAssembly; 
} 
partvehicle_c:> vehicle_b{ // a part reuses the parts tree by subsetting another part 
parteng:>engine{ 
partcy:>cyl[4]; 
} 
}

###### Reusing the parts tree via the view palette

To reuse the parts tree via the view palette

1. In a view, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating parts' space='UAF2P'] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='UAF2P'] the part whose decomposition you want to reuse.
2. In the view palette, under the Specializations group, click the subsetting button.
3. Click on the part you want to denote as the subsetting one and then click on the part you want to denote as the subsetted (reused) one.

###### Displaying the parts tree reuse in a view

To display the parts tree reuse in a view

1. In a view, select the subsetting and the subsetted part usage symbols, then u se the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='UAF2P'] command under the In-Between Relationships category . The subsetting relationship is displayed.
2. (Optional) To display owned features of the subsetting or subsetted parts, use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='UAF2P'] command. The owned features (part usages) are displayed. [ATTACHMENT filename='Parts tree_reuse_and_modification.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

using the feature typing (<em>defined by</em>) relationship.</p><h4>Decomposing part definitions via the Textual Editor</h4><p>To decompose part definitions via the Textual Editor</p><hr /><ol><li>In part usage, owned by a part definition, defined by another part definition <br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">fuelTank</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">FuelTank</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">}</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">def</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">RearAxleAssembly</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">differential</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Differential</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">rearAxle</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Axle</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">rearWheel1</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Wheel</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">rearWheel2</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Wheel</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Displaying decompositions of part definitions in a view</h4><p>To display decompositions of part definitions in a view</p><hr /><ul><li>In a view, select the part definition symbol and the part usage symbol it defines, then use the <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Display Specializations]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> </strong>command under the <strong>In-Between Relationships category</strong>. <br />The feature typing relationship is displayed.</li></ul><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Displaying_the_decomposition_of_a_part_definition_in_a_diagram.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Part decomposition" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Decomposing part usages</h3><p>You can display a parts tree representing a composition of a part from other parts.</p><h4>Decomposing part usages via the Textual Editor</h4><p>To decompose part usages via the Textual Editor</p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">In the Textual Editor, within the body of the part you want to decompose, </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating parts" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[create the new part usages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. </li><li>Click the <strong>Synchronize<span> </span></strong>button.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="698a77ad-c59e-4286-9cdd-a7902f3f2cf1"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">vehicle_b <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">: <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Vehicle</span></span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> {                                    <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// the owning part usage with nested part usages</span></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">engine</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Engine</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">        </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">cyl</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Cylinder</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    }</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">transmission</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Transmission</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">fuelTank</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">FuelTank</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">rearAxleAssembly</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">AxleAssembly</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Decomposing part usages via a view by creating a composite part</h4><p>To decompose part usages via a view by creating a composite part</p><hr /><ol><li>In a view, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating parts" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[create]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> or <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[display]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> the part for which you want to create a composite part.</li><li>Create a composite part in one of the following ways:<ol><li>Via the view palette (part symbol option):<ol><li>In the view palette, under the parts group, click the <strong>part</strong><span> button to create a part usage element.</span></li><li>Click on the part symbol displayed in the view pane to create a part owned by another. </li></ol></li><li>Via the view palette (feature membership option):<ol><li>In the view palette, under the Other Relationships group, click the <strong>feature membership</strong> <span>button.</span></li><li>First, click on the part symbol displayed in the view pane and then click <span>elsewhere</span> in the view pane to create a part owned by another.</li></ol></li><li>Via the smart manipulator (feature membership option):<br /><ol><li>Click the part symbol for which you want to create a composite part.</li><li>In its smart manipulator, click the <strong>feature membership</strong> icon<span>, then click elsewhere in the view pane </span>to create a part owned by another.</li></ol></li></ol></li></ol><h4>Decomposing part usages via a view by designating an existing part as a composite one</h4><p>To decompose part usages via a view by designating an existing part as a composite one</p><hr /><ol><li>In a view, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating parts" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[create]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> or <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[display]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> the parts you want to designate as the owner and the owned.</li><li>Designate an existing part as owned by another in one of the following ways:<ol><li>Via the view palette (feature membership option):<ol><li>In the view palette, under the Other Relationships group, click the <strong>feature membership</strong> <span>button.</span></li><li>First, click on the part symbol you want to designate as the owner, then the one you want to designate as the owned one.</li></ol></li><li>Via the smart manipulator (feature membership option):<br /><ol><li>Click the part symbol you want to designate as the owner.</li><li>In its smart manipulator, click the <strong>feature membership</strong> icon, then click on the part you want to designate as the owned one.</li></ol></li></ol></li></ol><h4>Displaying decompositions of part usages in a view</h4><p>To display decompositions of part usages in a view</p><hr /><ul><li>In a view, select the owning part usage symbol and use the <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Display Features]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> </strong>command under the <strong>Tree-View Style</strong> category. <br />The feature membership and the owned features (part usages) are displayed. </li></ul><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Decomposing_a_part_usage .png"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Part decomposition" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Reusing and modifying the parts tree</h3><p>A part can subset another part, facilitating parts tree reuse and modification. The subsetting part inherits the part decomposition and other features. You can then modify the inherited parts and features through redefinition and subsetting in addition to adding new parts.</p><h4>Reusing and modifying the parts tree via the Textual Editor</h4><p>To reuse and modify the parts tree via the Textual Editor</p><hr /><ol><li>In the Textual Editor, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating parts" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[declare the part usage]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> you want to reuse, then <ac:link ac:anchor="Subsetting a usage via the Textual Editor"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Subsetting" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[subset]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> it.</li><li>(Optional) <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Redefinition" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Redefine]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> or <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Subsetting" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[subset]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> the part features in the element body.</li><li>Click the <strong>Synchronize<span> </span></strong>button.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8811e49e-ed80-4671-a9c5-db96a9276d25"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">vehicle_b</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Vehicle </span>{<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">                                     <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// subsetted part</span></span></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">engine</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Engine</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">        </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">transmission</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Transmission</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">fuelTank</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">FuelTank</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">AxleAssembly</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">}</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">vehicle_c</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> :&gt; <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">vehicle_b </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">{                               <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// a part reuses the parts tree by subsetting another part</span></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">eng</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> :&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-background-accent-green-bolder,#1f845a);">4</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">];</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    }</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Reusing the parts tree via the view palette</h4><p>To reuse the parts tree via the view palette</p><hr /><ol><li>In a view, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating parts" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[create]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> or <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[display]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> the part whose decomposition you want to reuse. </li><li>In the view palette, under the Specializations group, click the <strong>subsetting </strong>button.</li><li>Click on the part you want to denote as the subsetting one and then click on the part you want to denote as the subsetted (reused) one.</li></ol><h4>Displaying the parts tree reuse in a view</h4><p>To display the parts tree reuse in a view</p><hr /><ol><li>In a view, select the subsetting and the subsetted part usage symbols, then u<span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">se the </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Display Specializations]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> </strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">command under the </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">In-Between Relationships category</strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">. The subsetting relationship is displayed.</span></li><li>(Optional) To display owned features of the subsetting or subsetted parts, use the <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Display Features]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> </strong>command. The owned features (part usages) are displayed.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Parts tree_reuse_and_modification.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Part decomposition" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=298093689 space=UAF2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00119: (2026x) Part interconnection

- page_id: `298093689`
- space_key: `UAF2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF2P/pages/298093689/2026x+Part+interconnection
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-12-03T08:54:06.215+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Structure > (2026x) Parts
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Parts can be connected to define their interactions and relationships within a model. The primary methods for connecting parts are:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Connection' space='UAF2P'] (i.e., without ports) that specify a higher level of abstraction, likely establishing physical or logical connections.
  - Parts may also be connected using:
    - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Flow' space='UAF2P'] connections
    - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Binding' space='UAF2P'] connections

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Parts connected via a connection.

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Interface' space='UAF2P'] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Port' space='UAF2P'] (i.e., with ports) that specify more detailed, formalized interactions.
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Port' space='UAF2P'] on parts must be compatible with the interface definition typing the interface usage that connects the parts via ports.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Parts connected through ports via an interface.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Parts can be connected to define their interactions and relationships within a model. The primary methods for connecting parts are: </p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Connection" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Connections]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> (i.e., without ports) that specify a higher level of abstraction, likely establishing physical or logical connections. <ul><li>Parts may also be connected using: <ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Flow" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Flow]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> connections</li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Binding" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Binding]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> connections</li></ul></li></ul></li></ul><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="display_connection.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Part interconnection" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>Parts connected via a connection.</h6><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Interface" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Interfaces]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Port" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[ports]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> (i.e., with ports) that specify more detailed, formalized interactions.<ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Port" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Port]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> on parts must be compatible with the interface definition typing the interface usage that connects the parts via ports.</li></ul></li></ul><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="display_interface.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Part interconnection" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>Parts connected through ports via an interface.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=298093668 space=UAF2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00120: (2026x) Parts

- page_id: `298093668`
- space_key: `UAF2P`

This section provides procedures for modeling parts.

<p>This section provides procedures for modeling parts. </p><p><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=298093841 space=UAF2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00121: (2026x) Perform action

- page_id: `298093841`
- space_key: `UAF2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF2P/pages/298093841/2026x+Perform+action
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-12-17T15:37:05.002+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Behavior > (2026x) Actions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A perform action usage specifies that an action is performed by the owner of the performed action usage. A perform action usage is referential, which allows the performed action behavior to be defined in a different context than that of the performer.

A perform action usage can be a feature of:

- a part definition or usage, specifying that the referenced action is performed by the containing part.
- an action definition or usage, whereby the perform action usage represents a referential "call" from the containing action to the performed action.

There are two ways an action can be performed by another element:

50%

- by referencing the action usage defined elsewhere in the model.
  - The element symbol is displayed with the perform keyword (unnamed element) or the perform action keyword (named element). [ATTACHMENT filename='perform_symbol.png'] [ATTACHMENT filename='perform_action_symbol_2.png']
  - See the procedures:
    - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Perform action' space='UAF2P'] :
      - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Perform action' space='UAF2P']
      - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Perform action' space='UAF2P']
      - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Perform action' space='UAF2P']

50%

- by containing an owned perform action usage, i.e., the performed action is the perform action usage itself.
  - The element symbol is displayed with the perform action keyword. [ATTACHMENT filename='perform_action_symbol.png']
  - See the procedures:
    - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Perform action' space='UAF2P'] :
      - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Perform action' space='UAF2P']
      - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Perform action' space='UAF2P']
      - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Perform action' space='UAF2P']

##### Performing an action by referencing an action usage defined elsewhere in the model

###### Performing actions via the Textual Editor1Performing actions via the Textual Editor

To perform actions via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, within the body of the element that you want to perform an action, do one of the following:
  1. For an unnamed perform action, declare the perform keyword followed by the name of the performed action.
  2. For a named one, declare the perform action keyword, specify the element name, then [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Reference subsetting' space='UAF2P'] the performed action.
2. Click the Synchronize button.

action 'run mission'; // referenced action

part'drone in any mission'{

// unnamed perform action referencing an action defined elsewhere in the modelperform'run mission';

// named perform action referencing an action defined elsewhere in the modelperformaction'run delivery mission' references'run mission';performaction'run delivery mission'::>'run mission';

}

###### Performing actions via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

To perform actions via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

- Drag an action element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Drag and Drop handler' space='UAF2P'] and drop it on:
  - the target element symbol that you want to perform the action by referencing it using the **Create Perform Action** command.
  - the perform action usage symbol that you want to perform the action by referencing it using the **Set Performed Action** command.

###### Performing actionsvia the Specification panel

To perform actions via the Specification panel

- Drag and drop an action element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Drag and Drop handler' space='UAF2P'] and drop it on the **Performed Action** field in the Properties section of the Specification panel for the perform action element. The appropriate action is specified for the element.

##### Performing an action via the owned perform action usageitselfas the performed action

###### Performing actions via the Textual Editor

To perform actions via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, within the body of the element that you want to perform an action, declare the perform action keyword followed by its name.
2. Click the Synchronize button.

part'drone in any mission'{

// performed action is the defined perform action usage itself contained by the parentperformaction'run mission';

}

###### Performing actions via the Create Element command

To perform actions via the Create Element command

1. In the Containment tree, right-click an element, and in the shortcut menu, click **Create Element**.
2. In the Create Element dialog, select the perform action element.
3. Name the element.
4. Specify the perform action's features.

###### Performing actions via the view palette

To perform actions via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Actions group, click the perform action button.
2. Click in the view pane where you want to create the perform action symbol.
3. Name the element.
4. Specify the perform action's features.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=298093712 space=UAF2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00122: (2026x) Port

- page_id: `298093712`
- space_key: `UAF2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF2P/pages/298093712/2026x+Port
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-12-03T08:54:54.145+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Structure
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A port is a connection point through which a part definition makes some of its features available in a limited way. A port definition defines features that can be made available via ports. Two ports are compatible for connection if they have directed features that match with inverse directions, which can be established using port conjugation.

##### Creating port usage and definition elements

###### Creating port usage and definition elements via the Textual Editor

To create port usage and definition elements via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the keyword:
  1. *port def*for a port definition.
  2. port for a port usage.
2. Specify the element name.
3. (Optional) Specify the port features in the element body.
4. Click the Synchronize button.

portdefOnOffCmdPort{outonOffCmd:OnOffCmd;}portonOffCmdPort:OnOffCmdPort;

###### Creating port usage and definition elements via the Create Element command

To create port usage and definition elements via the Create Element command

1. In the Containment tree, right-click an element, and in the shortcut menu, click **Create Element**.
2. In the Create Element dialog, select one of the following:
  1. port for port usage.
  2. port def for port definition.
3. Name the element.
4. (Optional) Specify the port's features.

###### Creating port usage and definition elements via the view palette

To create port usage and definition elements via the view palette

- To create an interface definition:
  1. In the view palette, under the Items/Ports/Attributes group, click the **port** def button to create an interface definition element.
  2. Click in the view pane where you want to create the port definition symbol.
- To create an interface usage:
  1. In the view palette, under the Items/Ports/Attributes group, click the **port** button to create an interface usage.
  2. In a view, click on an element symbol for which you want to create a port. If the needed button is not displayed, click the down-facing arrow next to either the **port**or the **port****def**button and select the needed button in the dropdown menu.

###### Creating port usage elements via the smart manipulator in a view

To create port usage elements via the smart manipulator in a view

- In a view, select an element symbol, and in its smart manipulator, click the port command. A port is created for the element.

###### Creating port usage elements via the symbol compartment in a view

For more information see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Compartments' space='UAF2P'] page.

To create port usage elements via the symbol compartment in a view

- Do one of the following:
  - Select an element symbol and click the Create Element button on it. In the dropdown menu, click the port element.
  - Select an element symbol and on the right side of the ports compartment, click the Create port button. The new element is created and displayed in the ports compartment.

###### Creating port usage elements via the Specification panel

To create port usage elements via the Specification panel

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specification panel' space='UAF2P'].
2. Do one of the following:
  1. In the Properties section bar, click the **Create Element** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The shortcut menu appears. Click the **port**element in the shortcut menu.
  2. In the **ports**compartment section's bar, click the **Create port**[IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The element is created in the **ports** compartment.

###### Auto-creating port definition elements via the Extract Definition command

To auto-create a port definition element via the Extract Definition command

- See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Extract Definition and Usage' space='UAF2P']page.

##### Conjugating ports

Every port definition has an implicit conjugate port definition that reverses input and output features. You can easily use the conjugate port definitions by prepending the ~ symbol to the port definition name.

###### Using conjugated ports definitions via the Textual Editor

To use conjugate port definitions via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want the element to be created and declare the port keyword.
2. (Optional) Follow with the port name.
3. Follow with the feature typing relationship.
4. Specify the conjugated port definition:
  1. Full conjugation notation:
    1. Port definition name followed by :: (qualified name notation), followed by the port definition name with the ~ symbol prepended.
  2. Shorthand notation:
    1. Port definition name with the ~ symbol prepended.
5. Click the Synchronize button.

portdefFuelPort{ // Port definition 
attributetemperature:Temp; 
outitemfuelSupply:Fuel; 
initemfuelReturn:Fuel; 
} 
partdefFuelTankAssembly{ 
portfuelTankPort:FuelPort; 
} 
partdefEngine{ 
 port engineFuelPort : FuelPort::'~FuelPort'; //Conjugate port definition 
portengineFuelPort: ~FuelPort; // Using a ~ symbol on the port type is a shorthand for using the conjugate port definition 
}

##### Directed port features

A feature with a direction (in, out or inout) is a directed feature. Two ports are compatible for connection if they have directed features that match with inverse directions.

###### Creating directed port features

To create directed port features

1. In the Textual Editor, prepend the direction keyword - in , out , inout - to the feature.
2. Click the Synchronize button. portdefFuelOutPort{ 
attributetemperature:Temp; 
outitemfuelSupply:Fuel;// feature fuelSupply with direction out 
initemfuelReturn:Fuel;// feature fuelReturn with compatible direction in 
} 
portdefFuelInPort{ 
attributetemperature:Temp; 
initemfuelSupply:Fuel;// feature fuelSupply with compatible direction in 
outitemfuelReturn:Fuel;// feature fuelReturn with compatible direction out 
}

ac:macro-id="30044d15-f229-4626-9e04-2b2739874b7d"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">port</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> { </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">out</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">onOffCmd</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">OnOffCmd</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">}<br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">port</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">onOffCmdPort</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">OnOffCmdPort</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Creating port usage and definition elements via the Create Element command</h4><p>To create port usage and definition elements via the Create Element command</p><hr /><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the Containment tree, right-click an element, and in the shortcut menu, click <strong>Create Element</strong>.</p></li><li>In the <strong>Create Element</strong> dialog, select one of the following:<ol><li><strong>port </strong>for port usage.</li><li><strong>port def </strong>for port definition.</li></ol></li><li>Name the element.</li><li>(Optional) Specify the port's features.</li></ol><h4 class="auto-cursor-target">Creating port usage and definition elements via the view palette</h4><p>To create port usage and definition elements via the view palette</p><hr /><ul><li>To create an interface definition:<ol><li>In the view palette, under the Items/Ports/Attributes group, click the<span> <strong>port </strong></span><strong>def </strong><span>button to create an interface definition element.</span></li><li>Click in the view pane where you want to create the port definition symbol.</li></ol></li><li>To create an interface usage:<ol><li>In the view palette, under the Items/Ports/Attributes group, click the<span> <strong>port </strong></span><span>button to create an interface usage.</span></li><li>In a view, click on an element symbol for which you want to create a port.<ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="769c3a3a-7b27-471d-b151-165748c95ab6"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If the needed button is not displayed, click the down-facing arrow next to either the <span><strong>port </strong></span>or the <span><strong>port </strong></span><strong>def </strong>button and select the needed button in the dropdown menu.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol></li></ul><h4 class="auto-cursor-target">Creating port usage elements via the smart manipulator in a view</h4><p>To create port usage elements via the smart manipulator in a view</p><hr /><ul><li>In a view, select an element symbol, and in its smart manipulator, click the <strong>port </strong>command. A port is created for the element.</li></ul><h4>Creating port usage elements via the symbol compartment in a view</h4><p>For more information see the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Compartments" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Compartments]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</p><p>To create port usage elements via the symbol compartment in a view </p><hr /><ul><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li>Select an element symbol and click the<span> </span><strong>Create Element</strong><span> </span>button on it. In the dropdown menu, click the <strong>port </strong>element.</li><li>Select an element symbol and on the right side of the <strong>ports </strong>compartment, click the<span> </span><strong>Create <span>port </span></strong>button. <br />The new element is created and displayed in the <strong>ports </strong>compartment.</li></ul></li></ul><h4 class="auto-cursor-target">Creating port usage elements via the Specification panel</h4><p>To create port usage elements via the Specification panel</p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:link ac:anchor="Opening the Specification panel"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Specification panel" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Open the Specification panel]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Do one of the following:</span><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the Properties section bar, click the <strong>Create Element</strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="create_element_in_specification_pane_button.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Port" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> button. The shortcut menu appears. </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Click the <strong>port </strong>element in the shortcut menu.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the <strong>ports </strong>compartment section's bar, click the <strong>Create port </strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="create_element_in_compartment_via_specification_pane_button.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Port" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> button. <br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The element is created in the <strong>ports</strong> compartment.</span></li></ol></li></ol><h4>Auto-creating port definition elements via the Extract Definition command</h4><p><span>To auto-create a port definition element via the Extract Definition command</span></p><hr /><ul><li><span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Extract Definition and Usage" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Extract Definition and Usage]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">page.</span></span></li></ul><h3 class="auto-cursor-target">Conjugating ports</h3><p>Every port definition has an implicit conjugate port definition that reverses input and output features. You can easily use the conjugate port definitions by prepending the ~ symbol to the port definition name. </p><h4>Using conjugated ports definitions via the Textual Editor</h4><p>To use conjugate port definitions via the Textual Editor</p><hr /><ol><li>In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want the element to be created and declare the <em>port</em> keyword.</li><li>(Optional) Follow with the port name.</li><li>Follow with the feature typing relationship.</li><li>Specify the conjugated port definition:<ol><li>Full conjugation notation: <ol><li>Port definition name followed by :: (qualified name notation), followed by the port definition name with the ~ symbol prepended.</li></ol></li><li>Shorthand notation:<ol><li>Port definition name with the ~ symbol prepended.</li></ol></li></ol></li><li>Click the <strong>Synchronize </strong>button.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3f352e50-2d26-4ad2-9d11-50ce9277573f"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">port</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">def</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">FuelPort</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> {                                                         <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// Port definition</span></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">attribute</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">fuelSupply</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">fuelReturn</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">FuelTankAssembly</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">port</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">def</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Engine</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> {<br />    <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">FuelPort</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;                               <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Directed port features</h3><p>A feature with a direction (in, out or inout) is a directed feature. Two ports are compatible for connection if they have directed features that match with inverse directions.</p><h4>Creating directed port features</h4><p>To create directed port features</p><hr /><ol><li>In the Textual Editor, prepend the direction keyword - <em>in</em>, <em>out</em>, <em>inout</em> - to the feature.</li><li>Click the <strong>Synchronize </strong>button.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="77ae2334-7366-4b0a-877c-e7480662bac9"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span fuelSupply with direction out </span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">in</span><span
